## Plan (Source of Truth for Scope)

```yaml
module: a1-003
level: A1
sequence: 3
slug: consonant-sounds
version: '2.0'
title: Consonant Sounds
subtitle: "Приголосні звуки — Voiced, Voiceless, Hard, and Soft"
focus: grammar
pedagogy: PPP
phase: A1.1 [First Contact]
word_target: 1200
objectives:
- Learner masters sonorant consonants (Л М Н Р В)
- "Learner understands voiced/voiceless pairs (Б/П Д/Т З/С Ж/Ш Г/Х Ґ/К)"
- Learner knows the hard vs soft consonant distinction
- Learner can read words with all consonants
- "Key rule: Ukrainian voiced consonants do NOT devoice at word end (зуб = зу[б], not зу[п])"
content_outline:
- section: Вступ — Introduction
  words: 100
  points:
  - "Review: M1 gave you the alphabet, M2 mastered vowels. Today: the 22 consonant
    letters — how they're organized by sound production."
  - "All 10 vowels from M2 are available. Focus is on consonant pronunciation
    and classification."
- section: "Сонорні — Sonorant Consonants"
  words: 200
  points:
  - "Sonorants are the 'musical' consonants — voice dominates over noise.
    5 sonorants: Л М Н Р В. You already know Л М Н from M1."
  - "Р — the rolled/trilled R! Words: риба (fish), рука (hand). Practice the
    tongue-tip trill. Looks like English P but sounds completely different."
  - "В — a sonorant in Ukrainian — closer to English W than English V. Lips rounded,
    NOT teeth on lip. Words: вода (water), вовк (wolf)."
- section: "Дзвінкі та глухі пари — Voiced and Voiceless Pairs"
  words: 400
  points:
  - "Hand-on-throat test: voiced (дзвінкий) = throat vibrates. Voiceless (глухий)
    = only air. Each pair is identical mouth position, different voicing."
  - "Б/П — бабуся (grandma) / павук (spider). Б = voiced, П = voiceless."
  - "Д/Т — дім (house) / тато (M1 review). Д = voiced, Т = voiceless."
  - "З/С — зуб (tooth) / суп (soup). З = voiced, С = voiceless."
  - "Ж/Ш — жук (beetle) / шапка (hat). Ж = voiced sibilant (like 'zh' in 'measure'),
    Ш = voiceless (like English 'sh')."
  - "Г/Х — гора (mountain) / хліб (bread). Г is a soft throaty sound (voiced glottal
    fricative), NOT a hard 'g' like English 'go'. Х like German 'ch' in 'ach'."
  - "Ґ/К — ґанок (porch) / кіт (M1 review). Ґ IS the hard 'g' (like English 'go') —
    very rare (~400 native words). Removed from alphabet in 1933, restored 1990."
  - "CRITICAL RULE: voiced consonants stay voiced at word end! зуб = зу[б], NOT
    зу[п]. хліб = хлі[б], NOT хлі[п]. Different from Russian and German."
- section: "Тверді та м'які — Hard and Soft Consonants"
  words: 250
  points:
  - "Most Ukrainian consonants come in hard/soft variants. A consonant becomes soft
    (palatalized) before І, Я, Ю, Є or when followed by Ь (covered in M4)."
  - "Examples: ліс (soft Л), день (soft Д and Н), сіль (soft С and Л)."
  - "Compare: лук (hard Л — bow (weapon)) vs люк (soft Л — hatch). The consonant changes,
    not the vowel!"
  - "Always-hard: Ж, Ш are always hard. Й is always soft. Details on Ь (the soft
    sign that forces softening) in M4."
- section: "Читання — Reading Practice"
  words: 150
  points:
  - "Words using the full consonant inventory. All vowels from M2 available."
  - "NO full sentences with verbs — grammar is not taught yet. Use word groups,
    noun phrases, and labeling: Це дім. Це хліб. Ось бабуся."
  - "Voiced/voiceless pair drills: зуб/суп, жук/шапка, гора/хор."
  - "Minimal pair practice: лук/люк (hard/soft), дим/дім (И/І)."
- section: "Підсумок — Summary"
  words: 100
  points:
  - "5 sonorants (Л М Н Р В), 6 voiced/voiceless pairs, hard/soft system."
  - "Self-check: What are the 5 sonorants? What is the voiceless partner of Б?
    Is Г a hard 'g' or a soft throaty sound? Do voiced consonants devoice at word end?"
  - "Next: M4 completes the alphabet — soft sign (Ь), apostrophe, affricates
    (Ц Ч Щ), digraphs (ДЖ ДЗ), and rare Ф."
vocabulary_hints:
  required:
  - "хліб (bread) — cultural staple; demonstrates Х; no-devoicing rule"
  - "зуб (tooth) — demonstrates З; no-devoicing drill with суп"
  - "дім (house) — high-frequency; demonstrates Д"
  - "вовк (wolf) — tale vocabulary; demonstrates В (sonorant)"
  - "жук (beetle) — demonstrates Ж; Bolshakova"
  - "шапка (hat) — demonstrates Ш; everyday clothing"
  - "гора (mountain) — demonstrates Г (throaty fricative); high-frequency"
  - "небо (sky) — demonstrates Н; high-frequency"
  - "рука (hand) — demonstrates Р (rolled); body vocabulary"
  - "бабуся (grandma) — demonstrates Б; high-frequency family word"
  recommended:
  - "павук (spider) — demonstrates П; Bolshakova"
  - "ґанок (porch) — demonstrates rare Ґ; classic textbook word"
  - "сіль (salt) — demonstrates soft С and Л; everyday kitchen word"
  - "люди (people) — demonstrates soft Л; high-frequency"
  - "суп (soup) — voiceless pair drill with зуб; everyday food"
  - "вода (water) — demonstrates В; high-frequency"
  - "лук (bow (weapon)) — hard Л; minimal pair with люк"
  - "люк (hatch) — soft Л; minimal pair with лук"
activity_hints:
```

## Activities YAML

```yaml
- type: watch-and-repeat
  title: Consonant Pronunciation Practice
  instruction: Watch each video, then repeat the sound and example word aloud. Focus
    on the difference between voiced and voiceless pairs.
  items:
  - letter: Р
    word: риба
    video: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=fMGsQ5KPQgg
    note: Rolled/trilled R — tongue tip taps behind upper teeth. Looks like English
      P but sounds completely different!
  - letter: В
    word: вода
    video: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=aFcvYfvQ2X4
    note: Closer to English W — lips round gently, no teeth on lip
  - letter: Б
    word: бабуся
    video: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=V1hxBE_JbGg
    note: Voiced — throat vibrates
  - letter: П
    word: павук
    video: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=JksSjjxyW5Y
    note: Voiceless partner of Б — same lip position, no vibration
  - letter: Д
    word: дім
    video: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=g4Bh-lqzd48
    note: Voiced — tongue behind upper teeth
  - letter: Т
    word: тато
    video: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=m-jcLR_gK0k
    note: Voiceless partner of Д
  - letter: З
    word: зуб
    video: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=BhASNxitC1A
    note: Voiced — like English Z
  - letter: С
    word: суп
    video: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=7UsFBgSL91E
    note: Voiceless partner of З — like English S
  - letter: Ж
    word: жук
    video: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=dIrGVcqPwqM
    note: Voiced sibilant — like ZH in English measure
  - letter: Ш
    word: шапка
    video: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=1D-6MIw3OXY
    note: Voiceless partner of Ж — like SH in English ship
  - letter: Г
    word: гора
    video: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=gVnclpSI0DU
    note: Soft throaty sound — NOT a hard G like English go
  - letter: Х
    word: хліб
    video: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=vpr58zJSJKc
    note: Voiceless partner of Г — like CH in German ach
- type: classify
  title: Sort the Consonants
  instruction: Drag each consonant letter into the correct category. Sonorants are
    the musical consonants. Voiced consonants make your throat vibrate. Voiceless
    consonants use only air.
  categories:
  - label: Sonorants
    symbol_hint: sonorant
    items:
    - Л
    - М
    - Н
    - Р
    - В
  - label: Voiced (paired)
    symbol_hint: voiced
    items:
    - Б
    - Д
    - З
    - Ж
    - Г
  - label: Voiceless
    symbol_hint: voiceless
    items:
    - П
    - Т
    - С
    - Ш
    - Х
- type: image-to-letter
  title: What Letter Does It Start With?
  instruction: Look at the picture and tap the consonant letter that the Ukrainian
    word starts with.
  items:
  - emoji: 🪲
    answer: Ж
    distractors:
    - Ш
    - З
    - Г
    note: жук (beetle)
  - emoji: 🧢
    answer: Ш
    distractors:
    - Ж
    - С
    - Х
    note: шапка (hat)
  - emoji: ✋
    answer: Р
    distractors:
    - Л
    - В
    - Н
    note: рука (hand)
  - emoji: 🏔️
    answer: Г
    distractors:
    - К
    - Х
    - Ґ
    note: гора (mountain)
  - emoji: 🐺
    answer: В
    distractors:
    - Р
    - Л
    - Б
    note: вовк (wolf)
  - emoji: 🍞
    answer: Х
    distractors:
    - Г
    - К
    - Ш
    note: хліб (bread)
  - emoji: 🏠
    answer: Д
    distractors:
    - Т
    - Б
    - Г
    note: дім (house)
  - emoji: 🕷️
    answer: П
    distractors:
    - Б
    - В
    - Р
    note: павук (spider)
- type: match-up
  title: Match Voiced to Voiceless Partner
  instruction: Each voiced consonant has a voiceless partner. Match them — same mouth
    position, different voicing.
  pairs:
  - left: Б (voiced)
    right: П (voiceless)
  - left: Д (voiced)
    right: Т (voiceless)
  - left: З (voiced)
    right: С (voiceless)
  - left: Ж (voiced)
    right: Ш (voiceless)
  - left: Г (voiced)
    right: Х (voiceless)
  - left: Ґ (voiced)
    right: К (voiceless)
- type: quiz
  title: Consonant Knowledge Check
  instruction: Choose the correct answer for each question about Ukrainian consonants.
  items:
  - question: Which of these consonants is VOICED?
    options:
    - text: Б
      correct: true
    - text: П
      correct: false
    - text: С
      correct: false
    - text: Ш
      correct: false
    explanation: Б is voiced — your throat vibrates when you say it. П, С, and Ш are
      all voiceless.
  - question: What is the voiceless partner of Б?
    options:
    - text: П
      correct: true
    - text: Т
      correct: false
    - text: С
      correct: false
    - text: К
      correct: false
    explanation: Б and П are a voiced/voiceless pair — same lip position, different
      voicing.
  - question: What is the voiced partner of Т?
    options:
    - text: Д
      correct: true
    - text: Б
      correct: false
    - text: З
      correct: false
    - text: Ж
      correct: false
    explanation: Д and Т are a pair — tongue touches the same spot behind upper teeth.
  - question: What is the voiceless partner of З?
    options:
    - text: С
      correct: true
    - text: Ш
      correct: false
    - text: Х
      correct: false
    - text: П
      correct: false
    explanation: З and С are a voiced/voiceless pair.
  - question: What is the voiced partner of Ш?
    options:
    - text: Ж
      correct: true
    - text: З
      correct: false
    - text: Г
      correct: false
    - text: Д
      correct: false
    explanation: Ж and Ш are a sibilant pair — Ж buzzes, Ш hisses.
  - question: Which letter represents the hard G sound (like English 'go')?
    options:
    - text: Ґ
      correct: true
    - text: Г
      correct: false
    - text: К
      correct: false
    - text: Х
      correct: false
    explanation: Ґ is the rare letter for the hard G sound. Г is a soft throaty sound,
      not a hard G.
  - question: In the word зуб (tooth), how is the final Б pronounced?
    options:
    - text: As voiced [б]
      correct: true
    - text: As voiceless [п]
      correct: false
    - text: It is silent
      correct: false
    - text: As [в]
      correct: false
    explanation: Ukrainian voiced consonants do NOT devoice at word end. зуб is зу[б],
      not зу[п].
  - question: How is Ukrainian В pronounced?
    options:
    - text: Closer to English W — lips rounded, no teeth
      correct: true
    - text: Like English V — upper teeth on lower lip
      correct: false
    - text: Like English F
      correct: false
    - text: Like English B
      correct: false
    explanation: Ukrainian В is a sonorant — lips round gently, closer to W than V.
  - question: How is Ukrainian Г pronounced?
    options:
    - text: A soft throaty sound (voiced glottal fricative)
      correct: true
    - text: A hard G like English 'go'
      correct: false
    - text: Like English H
      correct: false
    - text: Like English K
      correct: false
    explanation: Г is a gentle, breathy sound with voice — not a hard G. The hard
      G is the rare Ґ.
  - question: Which consonants are ALWAYS hard, even before І?
    options:
    - text: Ж and Ш
      correct: true
    - text: Б and П
      correct: false
    - text: Д and Т
      correct: false
    - text: Л and Н
      correct: false
    explanation: Ж and Ш are always hard. Most other consonants become soft before
      І, Я, Ю, Є.
- type: classify
  title: Hard or Soft Consonant?
  instruction: 'In each word below, the first consonant is either hard or soft. Sort
    the words by the quality of their first consonant. Remember: consonants before
    І, Я, Ю, Є are soft.'
  categories:
  - label: Hard consonant
    symbol_hint: hard
    items:
    - лук
    - суп
    - зуб
    - вовк
    - дим
  - label: Soft consonant
    symbol_hint: soft
    items:
    - люк
    - сіль
    - день
    - ліс
    - дім
- type: true-false
  title: True or False?
  instruction: Decide whether each statement about Ukrainian consonants is true or
    false.
  items:
  - statement: Ukrainian Р sounds like English P.
    correct: false
    explanation: Р looks like English P but is a rolled/trilled R sound — completely
      different.
  - statement: Ukrainian В is pronounced closer to English W than English V.
    correct: true
    explanation: В is a sonorant in Ukrainian — lips round gently, no teeth touch
      the lip.
  - statement: Ukrainian Г sounds like the G in English 'go'.
    correct: false
    explanation: Г is a soft throaty sound (voiced glottal fricative). The hard G
      is the rare letter Ґ.
  - statement: Voiced consonants in Ukrainian become voiceless at the end of a word.
    correct: false
    explanation: Ukrainian voiced consonants stay voiced at word end. зуб = зу[б],
      not зу[п].
  - statement: Ж and Ш are always hard consonants.
    correct: true
    explanation: Ж and Ш never soften, even before І. They are always hard.
  - statement: Б and П are a voiced/voiceless pair.
    correct: true
    explanation: Б is voiced, П is voiceless. Same lip position, different voicing.
  - statement: Ґ is the most common letter in the Ukrainian alphabet.
    correct: false
    explanation: Ґ is the rarest letter — only about 400 native Ukrainian words use
      it.
  - statement: A consonant before the vowel І becomes soft.
    correct: true
    explanation: Consonants soften before І, Я, Ю, Є. For example, ліс has a soft
      Л.
- type: anagram
  title: Unscramble the Word
  instruction: Rearrange the letters to form a Ukrainian word from the lesson.
  items:
  - scrambled: Б І Л Х
    answer: ХЛІБ
  - scrambled: К В О В
    answer: ВОВК
  - scrambled: А К П А Ш
    answer: ШАПКА
  - scrambled: А К У Р
    answer: РУКА
  - scrambled: А Р О Г
    answer: ГОРА
  - scrambled: О Б Е Н
    answer: НЕБО
  - scrambled: А Д О В
    answer: ВОДА
  - scrambled: К У В А П
    answer: ПАВУК

```

---

## Research Notes

# Дослідження: Consonant Sounds

## State Standard Reference
§4.1.4: "Vowel and consonant sounds" (lines 577–584)
Alignment: This module directly addresses the A1 phonetics requirement — learners distinguish consonant categories (voiced/voiceless, sonorant, hard/soft) and understand the no-final-devoicing rule unique to Ukrainian.

---

## Vocabulary Frequency

| Word | Notes | Key collocations |
|------|-------|------------------|
| хліб (bread) | Cultural staple, extremely high-frequency; demonstrates Х vs Г contrast; no-devoicing showcase | свіжий хліб, білий хліб |
| зуб (tooth) | High-frequency body word; primary no-devoicing drill; pairs with суп for voiced/voiceless contrast | болить зуб |
| дім (house) | Ultra-high-frequency; demonstrates Д; pairs with тато (M1 review) | великий дім, рідний дім |
| рука (hand) | Body vocabulary; showcases the rolled Р — learners must distinguish from English R | права рука, ліва рука |
| бабуся (grandma) | Family vocabulary, emotionally salient; demonstrates Б; M1 connective | рідна бабуся |
| вовк (wolf) | Fairy-tale register; demonstrates В as sonorant/labial approximant (not English V); classic textbook word | сірий вовк |
| жук (beetle) | Bolshakova textbook word; perfect Ж showcase; child-friendly image | чорний жук |
| шапка (hat) | Everyday clothing; Ш showcase; voiced/voiceless pair with жук | тепла шапка |
| гора (mountain) | High-frequency; demonstrates Г as voiced glottal fricative — critical distinction from Ґ | висока гора |
| ґанок (porch) | Rare Ґ; classic textbook example for the restored letter; extremely rare in running text (~400 native words) | на ґанку |
| сіль (salt) | Kitchen/everyday; showcases BOTH soft С and soft Л in one word — ideal hard/soft demonstration | дрібна сіль |
| лук / люк | Minimal pair: лук (bow (weapon), hard Л) vs люк (hatch, soft Л); single consonant change, dramatic meaning shift | — |

---

## Cultural Hooks

1. **Скоромовка (tongue twister)**: "Жук жужжить над жабою жаб" — native Ukrainian tongue twister isolating Ж sound; textbooks use скоромовки for Ж/Ш drills (Kravtsova Grade 3 targets Ж/ДЖ/ДЗ pronunciation explicitly).
2. **Хліб is sacred**: In Ukrainian tradition, welcoming guests with хліб і сіль (bread and salt) is the highest symbol of hospitality. Both words appear in this module; pairing them creates a natural cultural anchor for the Х and С sounds.
3. **Вовк in folk tales**: The grey wolf (сірий вовк) appears in nearly every Ukrainian казка — Ivan Tsarevich, Mykyta the Fox. Children's familiarity with вовк across cultures makes it a memorable hook for the sonorant В.

---

## Common Learner Errors

1. **Final devoicing (зуб → \*зуп)** → Correct: зу[б]. English and German devoice final consonants; Ukrainian does NOT. This is the #1 phonological error. Kravtsova Grade 3 explicitly drills "дзвінкі приголосні в кінці слова" — textbook exercises target this exact pattern.
2. **Г pronounced as English hard 'g'** → Correct: Г = voiced glottal/velar fricative (like a voiced 'h'). English speakers hear Г and default to the hard stop [g]. Only Ґ is [g]. Kravtsova Grade 3 has a dedicated section: "Правильна вимова слів зі звуком [ґ]" — showing textbooks treat this as a known error point.
3. **В pronounced as English V (teeth on lip)** → Correct: В in Ukrainian is a bilabial approximant, closer to English W. Learners must keep lips together, no teeth contact. Especially before О/У: вовк, вода sound almost like "wowk", "woda."
4. **Soft consonant confusion (лук vs люк)** → Learners don't hear palatalization as a phonemic distinction. English has no hard/soft consonant contrast. The лук/люк minimal pair forces attention to the feature.

---

## Cross-References
- Builds on: a1-001 (Ukrainian Alphabet — М1), a1-002 (Vowel Sounds — М2)
- Prepares for: a1-004 (Completing the Alphabet — soft sign Ь, apostrophe, affricates ЦЧЩ, digraphs ДЖДЗ)

---

## Teaching Strategy

- **Discovery exercise**: Show learners three word pairs on cards: зуб/суп, жук/шапка, гора/хор. Ask: "In each pair, what changed — just one letter. Which is voiced, which is voiceless?" BEFORE explaining the voiced/voiceless system, let learners try the hand-on-throat test themselves. Then reveal the pattern. This mirrors how Kravtsova Grade 3 approaches consonant work: exercises that prompt "дослідження мовних явищ" (language phenomena investigation) before rule delivery.
- **Best context**: The kitchen/home environment works best — хліб, сіль, суп, дім are all high-frequency domestic words that load naturally onto sound demonstrations without needing verbs.
- **Contrastive pairs for disambiguation**:
  - Г vs Ґ: гора (mountain) vs ґанок (porch) — "Ukrainian Г is like a whispered/voiced H, Ґ is the rare imported hard G"
  - В vs English V: вовк — hold a finger in front of lips, feel the air (В) vs feel the vibration on lip (English V)
  - лук vs люк — same letters, different quality of Л; meaning changes completely

## Notes for Content Writing

- Module is in A1.1 (First Contact phase) — ZERO verbs allowed. All examples must be bare nouns, Це + noun, or noun phrases.
- Section headers must be bilingual: `## Сонорні — Sonorant Consonants` (M1-14 rule from A1 quick-ref).
- The hand-on-throat test is a **physical exercise** — describe it in English, let students try with a few examples in a boxed callout.
- The Ґ restoration story (removed 1933, restored 1990) is pedagogically useful but keep it brief — one sentence. Frame it as a fact about the letter's rarity, not as a deep historical lesson.
- Word budget note: the plan allocates only 100 words for the introduction — keep it to a single orienting paragraph bridging from М2 vowels.
- Minimal pair лук/люк is the cleanest hard/soft demonstration — anchor the whole soft-consonant section around it.

## Pre-Computed Metrics

```
Word count:       0 / 1200 (0.0%)
Activities:       8
```

---

## Your Task: Plan Adherence + Factual Verification

### PART A: Plan Adherence Check (MANDATORY)

The plan YAML above is the source of truth for what the module MUST contain. Check EVERY point systematically:

#### A1. Content Outline Points

For EACH section in `content_outline`, and for EACH `points` entry within that section:
1. Search the module content for evidence that this point is addressed
2. Classify as:
   - **COVERED** — the point is clearly addressed in the content. Quote the relevant sentence(s).
   - **MISSING** — the point does not appear in the content. This is a **MEDIUM** severity issue.
   - **PARTIAL** — some aspect is covered but key details are missing. Note what's missing.

**Output a checklist:**
```
### Plan Point Coverage
- Section "Вступ — Introduction":
  - [x] Point 1: "A1 immersion 10-50%..." — COVERED (line ~5: "...")
  - [ ] Point 2: "Cultural hook: Cyrillic script..." — MISSING
  ...
```

#### A2. Required Vocabulary

For EACH word in `vocabulary_hints.required`:
1. Search the module content (not just the word list section — the entire prose)
2. Check if it appears in the activities YAML (if provided in the content above)
3. Classify as:
   - **PRESENT** — found in prose AND activities
   - **PROSE ONLY** — in prose but not in any activity (LOW)
   - **MISSING** — not in prose at all (**HIGH** severity)

#### A3. Activity Hints

For EACH entry in `activity_hints`:
1. Check if an activity of that `type` exists
2. Check if the `focus` matches
3. Check if the `items` count is met
4. Flag missing activity types as **MEDIUM**

#### A4. Section Word Budgets

For EACH section in `content_outline` with a `words` target:
1. Estimate the word count of the corresponding content section
2. Flag sections >10% under their target as **MEDIUM**

#### A5. Pronunciation Videos (if plan has `pronunciation_videos`)

Check that video links from the plan are embedded in the content. Flag missing embeds as **MEDIUM**.

---

### PART B: Factual Verification

For EVERY grammar explanation, factual claim, cultural assertion, and historical reference in the module content:

1. **Search the reference sources above** for confirming or contradicting evidence
2. **Classify each claim** as one of:
   - **CONFIRMED [Tier 1]** — matches a reference source. Cite the source (chunk ID or source identifier) and quote both the module claim and the reference.
   - **DISCREPANCY [Tier 1]** — contradicts a reference source. HIGH severity. Cite both the module claim and the contradicting reference with chunk ID. Suggest the correct version.
   - **UNVERIFIED** — no reference coverage. Flag explicitly. Do NOT evaluate from your own knowledge. Do NOT mark as correct or incorrect — just "UNVERIFIED".

### Citation Enforcement

Every DISCREPANCY entry MUST include:
- Exact quote from the module content
- Exact quote from the reference source
- **Chunk ID or source identifier** (e.g., "Textbook, Grade 3, chunk_abc123" or "VESUM: lemma X")

Free-text citations with no chunk ID or source identifier carry ZERO weight and will be programmatically discarded.

### Special Checks

1. **Callout boxes** (`[!did-you-know]`, `[!myth-buster]`, `[!culture-note]`, `[!fun-fact]`): These are the #1 source of fabrication. Scrutinize every claim.
2. **Grammar rules**: Verify rule statements against textbook references. Flag overgeneralizations.
3. **Etymologies**: Verify word origins against references. Flag folk etymologies.
4. **Statistics and dates**: Flag unsourced numbers.
5. **VESUM failures**: Words not found in VESUM may be misspellings or Russianisms.

---

## Output Format

You MUST use these exact delimiters:

```
===FACTUAL_REVIEW_START===
## Plan Adherence Summary

**Content outline points:** N total, N covered, N missing, N partial
**Required vocabulary:** N total, N present, N missing
**Activity hints:** N total, N matched, N missing
**Plan Adherence Score:** X/10
(10 = all points covered; deduct 1 per MISSING content point, 2 per MISSING required word)

### Plan Point Coverage

[Checklist from Part A1 — every point with [x] or [ ] and evidence]

### Required Vocabulary Coverage

| Word | In Prose? | In Activities? | Status |
|------|-----------|---------------|--------|
| ... | YES/NO | YES/NO | PRESENT / PROSE ONLY / MISSING |

### Activity Hints Coverage

| Hint Type | Focus | Required Items | Found? | Actual Items |
|-----------|-------|---------------|--------|-------------|
| ... | ... | N | YES/NO | N |

### Missing Plan Points (Fix Targets)

[List each MISSING or PARTIAL point with the exact plan text and which section it belongs to.
These become fix targets in the SECTION_FIX block below.]

---

## Factual Verification Summary

**Total claims checked:** N
**Confirmed [Tier 1]:** N
**Discrepancies [Tier 1]:** N
**Unverified:** N
**Factual Alignment Score:** X/10

## Confirmed Claims

[List confirmed claims with source citations]

## Discrepancies

### Discrepancy 1: [brief title]
- **Module says:** "[exact quote from module]"
- **Reference says:** "[exact quote from reference source]"
- **Source:** [chunk ID / source identifier]
- **Severity:** HIGH
- **Suggested fix:** [what to change]

[... more discrepancies ...]

## Unverified Claims

[List claims with no reference coverage]

## Verdict

**Status:** PASS / FAIL
(FAIL if any HIGH severity discrepancy OR 3+ MISSING plan points found)
===FACTUAL_REVIEW_END===
```

If you find fixable discrepancies, also output FIND/REPLACE pairs:

```
===SECTION_FIX_START===
FILE: /Users/krisztiankoos/projects/learn-ukrainian/curriculum/l2-uk-en/a1/consonant-sounds.md
FIND:
[exact text from module with the error]
REPLACE:
[corrected text based on reference source]
---
[... more pairs, max 15 ...]
===SECTION_FIX_END===
```

---

## Boundaries

- Do NOT evaluate style, naturalness, or pedagogy — that is Pass 2's job
- Do NOT score dimensions (except Plan Adherence and Factual Alignment) — that is Pass 2's job
- Do NOT evaluate factual claims from your own knowledge — ONLY compare against provided references
- Plan adherence checking uses the plan YAML as ground truth — this is objective, not subjective
- Do NOT fabricate references — if no reference covers a claim, mark it UNVERIFIED
- Maximum 15 FIND/REPLACE pairs (prioritize MISSING plan points over minor factual fixes)
